title: Addiction: A Primer
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-18-2025 - Addiction - A Primer - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Biblical Anthropology: Why Humans Are Prone to Addiction
subpoints: Humans are dependent by design—created to depend on God for life and blessing
Since the fall, humanity strives for autonomy but the design for dependence remains
Suppressed God-given dependence redirects toward idols—the pattern described in Romans 1
Addiction is a demonstration of created dependence expressing itself apart from God

outline.title: A Biblical Definition of Addiction
subpoints: Addiction is an idolatrous relationship with a mood-changing experience
The ultimate source of addiction is not the substance or activity but the person's heart
The person's life centers around worship of a particular experience
An idol can make a voluntary slave—what begins as a choice can become a bondage

outline.title: Addiction as Disease: An Inaccurate Model
subpoints: Alcoholics Anonymous popularized the idea that addiction is a disease
Yet every treatment for addiction is moral, not medical—even secular approaches use talk therapy and behavioral change
There is no empirical evidence to support the addiction-as-disease model
The disease model makes genuine repentance impossible because you don't repent from being sick

outline.title: How Addicts Feel and the Prototypical Biblical Addictions
subpoints: Addicts feel trapped, hopeless, enslaved, and controlled by the object of addiction
The controlling object tells them how to live, think, and feel
The Bible emphasizes three categories of addiction: sex, food, and alcohol (including mind-altering drugs)
The list of potential task masters is constantly growing because we are skilled at finding new idols

outline.title: The Biblical View of Drunkenness as the Prototypical Addiction
subpoints: In Scripture, drunkenness is always called sin, never called sickness
Proverbs 23:29-35 depicts the captivating appeal, the irrationality, the cravings, and the unchanged behavior
Drunkenness belongs in lists of sins alongside sexual immorality, greed, and selfish ambition
The pattern of drunkenness is not different from the pattern of any other addiction—it is a question of lordship

outline.title: Why Calling Addiction Sin Is Good News
subpoints: Calling addiction a disease removes the possibility of repentance and genuine freedom
Calling addiction sin opens the door to repentance, faith, and the grace of Jesus Christ
A broader biblical view of addiction challenges us to examine the heart's idols rather than just the specific substance
The biblical solution addresses all addictions because it addresses the root—the corrupt heart that suppresses God

summary:
This Sunday School lesson is introduced as an introductory primer on addiction, prompted by a guest speaker's testimony about losing a child to drug addiction. Pastor Lyro acknowledges upfront that the lesson will not cover everything and recommends Edward Welch's books Addictions: A Banquet in the Grave and Blame It on the Brain for further study.

The lesson begins with biblical anthropology: humans were created to be dependent on God, and since the fall, that design for dependence redirects toward idols—the pattern Paul describes in Romans 1. This leads to a definition: addiction is an idolatrous relationship with a mood-changing experience. The ultimate source of addiction is not the substance but the person's heart, which centers itself around the worship of a particular experience.

Pastor Lyro then challenges the disease model of addiction, popularized by Alcoholics Anonymous. He argues that every treatment for addiction is moral rather than medical, that there is no empirical evidence for the disease model, and most significantly, that the disease model makes genuine repentance impossible. You cannot repent of being sick. This removes the most essential element of genuine freedom from addictive behavior.

The sermon surveys the Bible's view of drunkenness as the prototypical addiction, examining Proverbs 23:29-35 for its vivid depiction of the captivating appeal, the irrationality, and the unchanged behavior associated with addiction. Drunkenness appears in New Testament lists of sins alongside sexual immorality, greed, and selfish ambition—never categorized separately as a disease.

The lesson closes by arguing that a biblical understanding of addiction is actually good news: naming addiction as sin opens the door to the one solution that addresses the root cause, namely the idolatrous heart that suppresses God. The gospel of Jesus Christ provides the only framework that addresses all addictions, because it addresses the heart's fundamental rebellion and offers genuine repentance, forgiveness, and transformation.
transcript:
## Introduction

Jennifer Stone spoke for the mother-daughter banquet and she spoke about the pain of losing a child to drug addiction and how the Lord sustained her through that. And so that got me thinking—I had the opportunity to speak on this once in a Sunday school lesson, not part of a series, only thing like that. So what do I want to talk about? I'm going to talk about what the Scriptures say concerning addiction.

So consider this lesson a primer on addiction—an introductory lesson on the subject. What you put on your house before you paint is a primer. So it's not a lack of knowledge of English to call it a primer—that's how an introduction is called.

I'll tell you right off the bat this will be a frustrating lesson because we're not going to get through it. And even the things we're going to get through, you're not going to find satisfying—you're going to want more. And if that's the case it's easy to fix. Lots of books out there. I recommend especially one called Addictions: A Banquet in the Grave by Ed Welch, and then another one called Blame It on the Brain, also by Ed Welch.

Addictions are everywhere. And substance abuse is a major factor in the homeless crisis that we are facing. If you're not aware that we have a homeless crisis going on, just drive down Martin Luther King Way and you should be aware of it. All of us know at least one person who has received the label of addict. And as with everything else, the Bible has something to say about it.

Time permitting, we are going to consider 11 things on the subject of addiction. As I said earlier, even if we get through the 11 points, you will not be satisfied at the end because this is an introduction. But I think it'll be helpful in shaping how you should think about addiction.

## Biblical Anthropology: Humans Are Dependent by Design

In order to clarify addiction, we need to start with biblical anthropology—the study of humanity according to the Scriptures. The Scriptures teach that man is dependent by nature. Humans are dependent by design—it's part of our design to be dependent. God created us to be dependent on him. God created man to live dependently on God in order to find life and blessing.

But since the fall, man strives for autonomy and self-sufficiency—there's a rebellion against God. We want to be a law unto ourselves. We want to be sufficient to ourselves. And yet our created design keeps popping up: we need to depend on something or someone besides ourselves.

This dependence then turns to other things—the idols that Romans 1 talks about. And addiction is a demonstration or illustration or result of our created design expressing itself in a sinful way.

According to the Scriptures, man is captivated and enslaved by the idols he trusts and loves. That results in addiction. Why do people become dependent? Because they are dependent. We're made to be dependent—dependent on God. The fall messed that up. So we suppress that design. And that creates the condition for addiction.

## A Biblical Definition of Addiction

What would be a biblical definition of addiction? Addiction is an idolatrous relationship with a mood-changing experience. Addiction is an idolatrous relationship—I mean idol as in IDOL, not IDLE—a relationship with a mood-changing experience.

Usually addiction happens because a substance or activity is used because the person wants to change how he or she feels. And anyone can become an addict and anything can become an addictive substance when you look at that definition.

The ultimate source of addiction is not the substance or the activity. It is the person. The person's life centers around worship of that particular experience once they engage in whatever substance or activity they are pursuing. An idol can make what begins as a voluntary relationship into a slavery relationship.

Because this is the biblical definition of addiction, addiction as a disease is an inaccurate model. Alcoholics Anonymous and all the other twelve-step programs that go with it promote the idea that addiction is a disease.

But think about how it is treated in an AA environment. It's talk therapy. Somehow you have a bodily disease that is healed by talking to people. That doesn't seem to fit what a disease is.

Every treatment for addictions is moral, not medical. No matter what you believe about addiction. You can go to a detox facility and spend 21 days to get chemically free from a substance. But the next step in the treatment is: okay, this is what you need to do to change. This is the accountability work. So it's moral even if you don't believe it's moral. It's moral, not medical.

And regardless of popular science, there's no empirical evidence to support the addiction as a disease model. And then the presence of multiple unrelated addictions also damages the disease model—is it one disease or not? But the most important thing that the disease model undermines is that it makes genuine repentance impossible. You don't repent from being sick. The disease model removes completely the possibility of repentance, and that removes one of the most essential elements to actually turn away from that behavior and turn to Christ.

## How Addicts Feel

How do addicts feel? Addicts feel as if they are trapped, out of control. They feel like hopeless, helpless worshipers devoted to something that can be very dangerous. They feel desperate. They feel a hunger and thirst for something. They feel like they can't let go even when their bad behavior yields very few pleasures and a great deal of pain—the cost-benefit analysis doesn't work.

Something or someone other than the living God controls them, and the controlling object tells them how to live, how to think, and how to feel. Alcohol tells you to place your own desires above the interest of your family. Pornography and other forms of sexual immorality tell you that the immediate pleasures are worth it. Gambling tells you that you might hit the big one on the next spin of the wheel. Food says one more serving, you can always purge.

In the Bible there are three categories that are emphasized as addictions: sex, food, and alcohol, which includes any mind-altering drugs. These are the most common addictions in the Scriptures. Yet the list of potential taskmasters is constantly growing because we're good at finding new idols.

What unites most of these activities or substances is that they deliver a bodily experience. With them we feel more alert, more distracted, calmer, less shy, or more powerful. Most addictions also change our physical experience very quickly. People are rarely addicted to vitamins because vitamins take a long time to work. These things work within seconds or minutes.

## The Biblical View of Drunkenness

The biblical view of drunkenness is that it is always called sin, never called sickness.

Proverbs 23: Who has sorrow? Who has strife? Who has complaining? Who has wounds without cause? Who has redness of eyes? Those who tarry long over wine, those who go to try mixed wine. Do not look at wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup and goes down smoothly. At the last it bites like a serpent and stings like an adder. Your eyes will see strange things and your heart utter perverse things. You will be like one who lies down in the midst of the sea, like one who lies on the top of a mast. "They struck me," you will say, "but I was not hurt; they beat me, but I did not feel it. When shall I wake? I must have another drink."

You can see here the captivating appeal, the irrationality, the cravings that seem irresistible, and the fact that the bad behavior concerning this is not changed in the drinker.

The pattern of drunkenness is not different from sexual immorality, severe greed, or selfish ambition. It is far more consistent to say that drunkenness is an addiction while also saying that stealing is an addiction, that sexual sin is an addiction, that being selfish is an addiction—because the Bible always puts those together in lists of sins. In 1 Corinthians 5:11 Paul says, I am writing to you not to associate with anyone who bears the name of brother if he is guilty of sexual immorality, greed, or is an idolater, reviler, drunkard, or swindler. Drunkard is always present with the other sins. We see the same in 1 Corinthians 6:9-10 and Galatians 5 with the works of the flesh.

When we look at it closely, drunkenness is a lordship problem. Any use of substance that recurrently alters your mind is a matter of lordship. Who is your master—God or your desires? Do you desire God above all else or do you desire something in creation more than you desire the Creator?

Heavy drinkers neither drink to glorify God nor to love their neighbor. They drink to indulge their own desires, whether those desires are pleasure, freedom from pain, freedom from fear, forgetting, or even vengeance.

## Why This Matters: The Heart Is the Root

Now we're starting to see the why of addictions: it is a corrupt heart that is self-centered, yet a heart that was created to find fulfillment in God. In its rebellion, it still needs to find fulfillment in something or someone else. The apostle teaches that in Romans 1—we've been created to worship God, and yet we suppress that truth. But it's so much a part of our design that every single molecule of our body, every strand of DNA, includes this desire to worship. We suppress that, but it's so powerful that it pops out in our idolatrous relationships in our lives. That's the why of addictions.

And that's why it's appropriate to link addiction with sin. When we look at it from this angle, the Bible is not silent on addiction. The solution that the Bible provides addresses all addictions, not just this particular addiction—are you with me? So we have a solution to all addictions, not just one particular one, because we understand the root.

The apostle Paul speaks of that in Romans chapter 7: for I know that nothing good dwells in me, that is in my flesh. For I have the desire to do what is right, but no ability to carry it out. For I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I keep on doing.

## Closing

We were able to get through number six of our list, so there are five that we're not going to cover today. But I hope this has at least whet your appetite. If you don't agree with what I'm saying, I pray that you would actually have a biblically-reasoned reason to disagree—not just "I don't agree with it" without a basis. Because if that's where you put yourself, you put yourself in a very dangerous place: disagreeing with what the Scriptures say while not being able to say exactly why.

I encourage you to keep on studying this subject. And we're going to keep on looking at how to help ourselves and others who are struggling with addiction at a different time.

Father in heaven, thank you that you are good. Thank you that your word is sufficient, and in principle, touches on everything. We pray that you help us to be submissive to it, for asking Jesus' name, amen.
